9: Ruth's request — ûpāraśtā kənāpəḵā ʿal ʾāmāṯəḵā kî gōʾēl ʾāttāh — 'spread your wing/skirt over your servant, for you are a redeemer' — is the book's most theologically charged sentence. The word kānāp deliberately echoes Boaz's prayer in Ruth 2:12: 'under whose wings you have come to take refuge.' Ruth is asking Boaz to fulfill what he prayed YHWH would be for her. The spreading of the garment corner over a woman is a formal gesture of betrothal (cf. Ezekiel 16:8: 'I spread my garment over you and covered your nakedness... you became mine') — both a legal act (signaling intention to redeem/marry) and a symbolic enactment of YHWH's sheltering care. Ruth simultaneously proposes marriage, invokes the gōʾēl obligation, and identifies Boaz as the human embodiment of YHWH's protective wing.
10: Boaz names Ruth's action as ḥeseḏ for the third and climactic time: heṭabt ḥasdēḵ hāʾaḥărôn min hāriʾšôn — 'you have made your last hesed better than the first.' The 'first' hesed is presumably Ruth's commitment to Naomi in Ruth 1; the 'last' (better) hesed is her seeking Boaz rather than younger men. The comparative heṭabt... min (made better than) is remarkable: Boaz rates Ruth's covenant loyalty as exceeding expectation — she chose the older, qualified redeemer rather than romance with someone younger. Hesed at its fullest is this: acting on covenant obligation even when another option would be personally easier. Boaz recognizes in Ruth the same quality that defines YHWH's own character (Exodus 34:6: rab ḥeseḏ).
12: Boaz's disclosure — wəgam yēš gōʾēl qārôḇ mimmennî — 'there is also a redeemer closer than I' — introduces the nearer kinsman who must first be given the option to redeem (Ruth 4:1-6). The legal process requires the nearest gōʾēl to have first refusal; Boaz cannot bypass him without violating the very law that legitimizes the redemption. This creates the narrative tension of Ruth 4: will the nearer gōʾēl redeem? The answer is no — he 'cannot redeem for himself, lest he ruin his own inheritance' (pᵉn-ʾašḥît ʾet naḥǎlātî, Ruth 4:6). The legal obstacle that nearly prevents the redemption is the narrative's way of making Boaz's willingness to bear the cost the central point of the book.
1: vatomer lah naomi chamotah biti halo avaqesh lach manoach asher yitav lach: 'Then Naomi her mother-in-law said to her: “My daughter, shall I not seek rest (manoach) for you, that it may go well with you?”' Manoach (resting place, security) is from the same root as menucha in v9 of Ruth 1 — Naomi's original prayer for the daughters-in-law (yiten YHWH lachem menucha) is now being answered through Naomi's own initiative. The structural echo is intentional: what Naomi prayed God would grant, she is now working to achieve, having recognized Boaz as the instrument. Prayer and human action collaborate; providence works through means.
2: veatah halo boaz meyuda'tenu asher hayit et-ne'arotav hineh hu zorea et-goren hase'orim halaylah: 'Now is not Boaz — our kinsman — with whose young women you have been? He is winnowing barley at the threshing floor tonight.' Meyuda'tenu (our known one, our kinsman) — Naomi uses the intimate kinship term, indicating the relationship that makes the plan possible. The goren (threshing floor) is a liminal space: the harvest boundary between agricultural labor and celebration. Winnowing at night, when the evening breeze came up, was standard practice. The threshing floor scene that follows takes place in this in-between space — harvest done but new life not yet established — between the old economy and the new.
3: verachatz vesakht vesamet simalotayich uyaradt hagoren: 'Wash yourself, put on fragrant oil, dress yourself in your best garment, and go down to the threshing floor.' Naomi's instructions have a deliberate sequence: washing, anointing (sukh, anoint with oil — a restoration act associated with the end of mourning, 2 Samuel 12:20), and dressing in one's 'garment' (best clothes). These are precisely the acts Ruth would have suspended during her widow's mourning. Naomi is instructing Ruth to resume the posture of an eligible woman. The scene is careful to avoid explicit sexual framing: Ruth is presenting herself as available for redemption, not for seduction. al-tivad'i la-ish ad kaloto le'echol velishhot: 'Do not make yourself known to the man until he has finished eating and drinking.' Wait for the right moment — a social wisdom instruction that also ensures Boaz is in a relaxed, receptive state.
4: vihi veshochvo veyada'at et hamakom asher yishkav-sham uba'at vegilleit marge'lotav ushachavt: 'When he lies down, take note of the place where he lies. Then go, uncover his feet, and lie down.' Margelot (feet, the place of the feet) — Ruth is to lie at his feet, the posture of a supplicant before a lord or a kinsman approaching a superior. The uncovering of the feet is a gesture loaded with symbolism: in ANE practice, uncovering the feet of a sleeping man was a way of indicating a claim. Ruth's action requests coverage — she will explicitly ask Boaz to spread his kanaf (wing/skirt) over her (v9). The two gestures — uncovering his feet, requesting he cover her — form the claim ritual. vehu yaggid lach et asher ta'asi: 'he will tell you what to do' — Naomi trusts Boaz to respond correctly once the situation is created.
5: vatomer eleiha kol asher tomri elai e'eseh: 'Ruth said to her: “I will do everything you say.”' Ruth's obedience to Naomi continues the pattern of her initial declaration (Ruth 1:16-17). Her words here echo the Israelite covenant formula from Sinai: kol asher dibber YHWH na'aseh (all that YHWH has spoken we will do, Exodus 19:8; Ruth 24:3). Ruth's total submission to Naomi's instruction is narrated without comment — but the reader familiar with covenant language recognizes the formula of complete loyalty being applied to human relationship.
6: vatired hagoren vattas kechol asher tzivatah chamotah: 'So she went down to the threshing floor and did everything her mother-in-law had instructed her.' The narrative economy is notable: everything Naomi said is executed without modification or deviation. Ruth's obedience is absolute and un-editorialized. The threshing floor is now the stage for the book's turning point — all the preceding scenes (the gleaning, the meals, the kinship identifications) have been preparatory. The legal and covenantal action now takes place at night, at the liminal boundary of harvest completion.
7: vayochal boaz vayesht vayitav libo vayavo lishkav biqtzeh ha'arema: 'When Boaz had eaten and drunk and his heart was glad (vayitav libo), he went and lay down at the far end of the grain heap.' Vayitav libo (his heart was good/glad) is a contentment phrase used throughout the OT (Judges 16:25; Ruth 19:6; 1 Samuel 25:36). Boaz is relaxed, the harvest is done, the season's work complete — he has no reason to expect what is about to happen. vatavo valat vategel margelotav vatishkav: 'she came quietly, uncovered his feet, and lay down.' The adverb valat (quietly, stealthily) marks the approach as deliberate and planned. Ruth executes Naomi's instructions precisely.
8: vayehi bachatzi hallaylah vayecherad ha-ish vayyilafet vehinneh ishah shochebet margelotav: 'At midnight the man was startled (vayecherad), turned himself (vayyilafet), and — look! a woman was lying at his feet.' Vayecherad (he trembled/was startled) describes the physical shock of waking to an unexpected presence. Vayyilafet (turned himself, twisted around) — an unusual verb, possibly describing the grasping motion of someone waking to something underfoot. The word hinneh (behold! look!) marks Boaz's moment of perception as a significant discovery. The entire scene is handled with narrative restraint: nothing happens beyond the stated facts, and the narrator refuses to fill in what might have occurred in the night between midnight and morning (v14).
11: ve'atah biti al-tire'i kol asher tomri elai e'eseh-lach ki yoda'a kol sha'ar ami ki eshet chayil at: 'And now, my daughter, do not be afraid. I will do for you everything you have asked, for all the gate of my people knows that you are a woman of worth (eshet chayil).' Eshet chayil (woman of valor/worth) is the feminine form of gibbor chayil used of Boaz in Ruth 2:1. The narrator has been building to this designation: Ruth's gleaning diligence, her loyalty to Naomi, her restraint at the threshing floor — all constitute the chayil that the town's testimony confirms. Boaz's statement that 'all the gate knows' is significant: the gate is the legal assembly. Ruth's reputation has preceded any formal proceedings. The eshet chayil designation also echoes the closing poem of Proverbs (Ruth 31:10-31), which begins eshet chayil mi yimtza (a woman of worth, who can find?) — Ruth is the narrative embodiment of that figure.
13: lini halaylah vehayah vaboqer im-yig'alech tov yig'al ve'im-lo yachpotz lig'olach gealticha anochi: 'Stay here tonight. In the morning — if he is willing to act as kinsman-redeemer for you, good, let him do it. But if he is not willing, as YHWH lives, I will redeem you.' The oath chay YHWH (as YHWH lives) is the strongest form of personal guarantee in biblical Hebrew — Boaz invokes YHWH's own life as the surety of his promise. His willingness to redeem is now bound by covenant oath. The nearer kinsman will refuse; Boaz will fulfill. Shichvi ad-haboqer: 'lie down until morning' — the night is protected, proper, and unscandalous; Boaz himself ensures Ruth's departure before she can be recognized (v14).
14: vatishkav margelotav ad haboqer vataqom biterem yakir ish et re'ehu: 'So she lay at his feet until morning. She rose before anyone could recognize another, for he said: “Let it not be known that a woman came to the threshing floor.”' Boaz's care for Ruth's reputation is active: he explicitly instructs that her presence be undisclosed. His reason is protective of Ruth, not of himself. The threshing floor at night was not a scandal-free location in Israelite social memory (cf. Hosea 9:1); Boaz takes no chances with Ruth's honor. The detail that 'she rose before anyone could recognize another' (biterem yakir ish et re'ehu) indicates pre-dawn darkness — the night has been honorable throughout, and Boaz ensures the morning will not suggest otherwise.
15: vayyomer havi hamitpahat asher alayich ve'itzeti-bah vatochel shesh midot se'orim: 'He also said: “Bring the cloak you are wearing and hold it out.” She held it out, and he measured six measures of barley into it.' Six measures of barley is a significant gift — large enough to burden Ruth for the walk home, conspicuous enough that Naomi will ask about it. The quantity may be deliberate: six units suggest work, while seven suggests completeness (cf. the six days of harvest labor and the seventh of rest). Boaz sends Ruth away laden, not empty-handed — a direct response to Naomi's complaint that YHWH brought her back 'empty' (Ruth 1:21). Each gift from Boaz is an anti-emptiness act.
16: vatavo el-chamotah vatomer mi at bitti vataged lah et kol asher asah lah ha-ish: 'When she came to her mother-in-law, Naomi asked: “Who are you, my daughter?” And she told her all that the man had done for her.' The question mi at (who are you?) has been read as recognition — 'who are you, now?' — asking whether Ruth has been transformed by the night's encounter. Ruth is not yet a wife, but she is no longer merely Naomi's Moabite companion. She is now a woman under the formal protective commitment of a goel. The answer to Naomi's question will unfold in Ruth 4.
17: vatomer shesh hase'orim ha'elle natan li ki amar elai al-tavo reqam el-chamotach: 'She added: “He gave me these six measures of barley, with the instruction not to go back empty-handed (reqam) to your mother-in-law.”' The word reqam (empty) directly echoes Naomi's lament in Ruth 1:21: 'YHWH has brought me back empty (reqam).' Boaz explicitly refuses to send Ruth back empty-handed to Naomi — his gift is targeted at reversing Naomi's stated emptiness. Whether or not Naomi understood the echoed word, the narrator and the reader do: the man who says 'not empty' is answering YHWH's supposed abandonment of Naomi.
18: vatomer shevi biti ad asher tede'i eich yippol davar ki lo yishqot ha-ish ki-im killah hadavar hayom: 'Then Naomi said: “Stay still, my daughter, until you know how the matter will turn out. The man will not rest unless he settles the matter today.”' Naomi's confidence in Boaz is complete: ki lo yishqot ha-ish ki-im killah hadavar (the man will not rest/be quiet unless he has completed the matter). Shaqat (to rest, be quiet, settle) echoes the manoach/menucha (rest) theme — Boaz will not find his own rest until he has secured Ruth's rest. The one who gives rest is himself driven by the need to give it. Naomi's instruction to Ruth — 'stay still' (shevi) — suspends Ruth's agency while the legal mechanism works. The waiting is itself an act of trust.
